perturbation.original_text: b. No other use or disclosure of confidential information. Except as permitted above, neither of us will use or disclose the other’s confidential information for five years after we receive it. The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.changed_text: b. No other use or disclosure of confidential information. Except as permitted above, neither of us will use or disclose the other’s confidential information for one year after we receive it. The one-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could violate trade secret laws, as many trade secrets retain value and require protection for longer periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act does not specify a maximum period of protection, but a one-year limit is unlikely to provide adequate protection for many trade secrets, potentially rendering the confidentiality agreement unenforceable for anything considered a trade secret under Washington law. The statute implies protection should last as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, potentially contradicting trade secret laws. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that trade secrets often require longer protection periods to maintain their value. The Law Explanation suggests that a one-year limit might be inadequate for many trade secrets under Washington law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, RCW 19.108.010, define 'trade secret' as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy. A one-year confidentiality period could be deemed insufficient to protect this value, especially if the trade secret's value extends beyond a year. The changed contract creates a contradiction because the curtailed confidentiality period undermines the necessary protection for information meeting the definition of a 'trade secret' under Washington law.

perturbation.original_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 10 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days could be considered unreasonable in certain business relationships, especially if it disrupts established contractual expectations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Washington)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Washington doesn't have a codified statute for good faith and fair dealing, it is implied in all contracts. Reducing the termination notice to 10 days might be a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if it deprives one party of the benefit of the bargain. What constitutes a 'reasonable' notice period often depends on the specific circumstances and industry standards, but 10 days can be too short, especially in complex agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 to 10 days, which the Law Explanation suggests might breach the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it deprives one party of the benefit of the bargain. The explanation indicates that a 'reasonable' notice period depends on circumstances, but 10 days 'can be too short', implying a violation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards,' suggesting that a drastically shortened notice period could be construed as failing to act in good faith if it unfairly disadvantages one party, thus creating a legal contradiction by violating an implied covenant.

perturbation.original_text: In any dispute relating to this agreement the prevailing party will be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any dispute relating to this agreement, each party will bear their own attorneys’ fees and costs, regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the attorney's fees clause may contradict Washington's Wage Rebate Act if the dispute involves non-payment of wages that would trigger this section.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 49.48.030 states that in any action brought to recover wages, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorney's fees and costs of suit. By stating that each party will bear their own fees regardless of outcome, this contract attempts to waive a right conferred by Washington State law, which would be impermissible in a wage dispute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 49.48.030: Attorney's fee in action on wages—Exception. Print RCWs > Title 49 > Chapter 49.48 > Section 49.48.030 49.48.020 << 49.48.030 >> 49.48.040 PDF RCW 49.48.030 Attorney's fee in action on wages — Exception. In any action in which any person is successful in recovering judgment for wages or salary owed to him or her, reasonable attorney's fees, in an amount to be determined by the court, shall be assessed against said employer or former employer: PROVIDED, HOWEVER, That this section shall not apply if the amount of recovery is less than or equal to the amount admitted by the employer to be owing for said wages or salary. [ 2010 c 8 s 12048 ; 1971 ex.s. c 55 s 3 ; 1888 c 128 s 3 ; RRS s 7596.]
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts RCW 49.48.030, which mandates that a prevailing party in a wage recovery action is entitled to reasonable attorney's fees. The changed text explicitly states that each party bears their own fees, regardless of who prevails. This eliminates the right to recover attorney's fees conferred by the statute, thereby creating a legal contradiction. The law snippet explicitly states: 'In any action in which any person is successful in recovering judgment for wages or salary owed to him or her, reasonable attorney's fees... shall be assessed against said employer or former employer.'

perturbation.original_text: We will not disclose the other’s confidential information to third parties
perturbation.changed_text: We may disclose the other’s confidential information to third parties if there is any potential benefit to us or an affiliate.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing disclosure of confidential information for any 'potential benefit' directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and potentially violates trade secret laws if it results in misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of a trade secret agreement, and opens the door to potential misappropriation if the definition of "benefit" is too wide. By allowing disclosure for any potential benefit, regardless of whether the benefit outweighs the harm to the other party, this essentially nullifies the entire confidentiality agreement and enables the other party to disclose the information, thereby potentially being an illegal act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clear contradiction by allowing disclosure of confidential information based on 'any potential benefit', which directly undermines the core purpose of confidentiality agreements and potentially violates trade secret laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both defining 'misappropriation' in RCW 19.108.010, include 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The original text ensured no disclosure, whereas the changed text permits it under a very broad condition, effectively removing the 'duty to maintain secrecy' mentioned in the definition of 'Improper means' in both snippets. This creates a scenario where trade secrets could be misappropriated, as any potential benefit could be used as justification for disclosure, thus failing to maintain secrecy as required by law.
